medications.indication_rationale: Once-daily potent topical steroid to suppress the acute contact dermatitis.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply thinly once daily to the affected area (as per source note)
10 mg orally daily (as per source note)

medications.indication_rationale: Acting as an additional irritant — must be stopped.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Avoid metal (nickel) contact with the skin — don't wear the offending belt buckle; if a belt is needed, tuck the shirt in as a barrier.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Removing the allergen is the definitive treatment.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Stop the OTC antiseptic cream and avoid scratching.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Removes an aggravating irritant and prevents excoriation/infection.

patient_education_points.point: This is an allergy to the metal (likely nickel) in your belt buckle — the rash is the exact shape of where it touches. Avoiding that contact is the key to healing.
Stop the antiseptic cream (it's irritating the skin), use the prescribed steroid ointment thinly once a day, and it should settle within a day or two.
If the redness spreads and gets hot beyond the square, turns to thick yellow pus, or you develop a fever — seek urgent care.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Resolution and long-term allergen avoidance; consider patch testing.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Spreading hot redness, pus or fever — seek urgent care.

must_include_items: Diagnose nickel/metal allergic contact dermatitis from the contact-shaped plaque
Cease the OTC antiseptic irritant and prescribe a potent topical corticosteroid
errors_of_commission: Prescribing an antifungal/antibiotic for presumed tinea/impetigo
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires allergen avoidance + ceasing the irritant + a topical steroid, with a secondary-infection safety-net; the definitive step is avoiding metal-skin contact.
